t: History of affective episodes without a medical cause.
Laboratory: TSH, B12, cortisol, ANA, kidney, liver.
Etiological treatment — correction of medical condition or medication.
Depressive — SSRIs (disease-specific selection; cardiac — sertraline, escitalopram; oncology — mirtazapine);
Manic — antipsychotic (quetiapine, olanzapine); in steroid-induced mania, taper steroids or use antipsychotic;
Change of medical condition or medication.
Choice appropriate to cardiac, oncological, neurological context.
Myth 1: "Depression is 'normal' for a patient with a medical illness"
Clinical depression worsens medical condition course; requires active intervention.
Myth 2: "Steroid-induced mania resolves on its own"
dose reduction or antipsychotic addition required.
thyroid correction first; antidepressants for residual symptoms.
HCV interferon therapy 20–40% — active monitoring required.
Levenson J.L. APA Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine 2018.
Robinson R.G., Jorge R.E. Post-stroke depression: a review. Am J Psychiatry 2016;173(3):221–231.
